citation_sentences: The main phase composition of the LDSs was analyzed by XRD, and the diffraction patterns are shown in Fig. 1. The diffraction peaks reveal a single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) austenitic structure, as confirmed by the presence of (111), (200), (220) and (311) peaks. These results indicate that the solution treatment preserves the austenitic matrix while modifying the relative peak intensities. Notably, the (111) peak intensity significantly increases following solution treatment, whereas the (220) peak intensity correspondingly decreases.

paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: The crystallographic characteristics of the LDSs were investigated through EBSD, and the results are shown in ${ \mathrm { F i g . ~ } } 3 ,$ and Fig. 4 summarizes the average grain size, kernel average misorientation (KAM) value, and GB density for all samples. The inverse pole figure (IPF) maps in Fig. 3bd reveal that LDSs do not exhibit significant preferred orientations after solution treatment and that the crystal structure orientations have a random crystallographic orientation distribution. Fig. 3a indicates that the S0 sample has a nonuniform grain size distribution, and some small grains are distributed at the interface of large grains. Solution treatment effectively homogenizes the grain size, as evidenced in Fig. 3b-d, and the grain size gradually increases with temperature. The maximum average grain size of 32.0 μm is achieved at the highest solution temperature of 1150 ℃ (Fig. 4a-d).

The KAM mapping was employed to qualitatively evaluate the distribution of local residual stresses within the materials, where elevated KAM values correspond to increased residual stress accumulation and consequently higher corrosion susceptibility[36]. Fig. $3 a _ { 1 } – \mathbf { d } _ { 1 }$ present the KAM distributions for the samples. The S0 sample exhibits the most pronounced residual stress, with an average KAM value of 1.62 as shown in Fig. 4e. After solution treatment, the sub-grain boundaries undergo dynamic recrystallization, reducing the residual stress in the S950, S1050, and S1150 samples to an average KAM value of approximately 0.4. The GB distribution analysis in Fig. 3a -d reveals that the S0 sample contains a substantial population of low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 2–10◦). After solution treatment, the S950, S1050, and S1150 samples exhibit complete recrystallization[37], characterized by abundant annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, Σ3, 60◦) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, >10◦) within equiaxed austenite grains. Fig. 4e indicates that the S950 sample has the maximum densities of both ATBs and HAGBs, which are 0.146 and 0.114 $\mu \mathrm { m } / \mu \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }$ , respectively. These boundary densities progressively decrease with increasing solution temperature, with the S1150 sample reaching minimum values of 0.040 and 0.019 μm/μm2 , respectively.

The surface corrosion morphology of the LDSs after potentiodynamic polarization to 5 ${ \mathrm { m A / c m } } ^ { 2 }$ is shown in Fig. 7. According to Fig. 7a, the S0 sample exhibits extensive corrosion damage, characterized by numerous deep pits resulting from elevated surface residual stress and a heterogeneous GB distribution (Fig. 3). After solution treatment at ${ 9 5 0 } ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C } ,$ , the LDS shows slight pitting corrosion (Fig. 7b), with smaller pit sizes, indicating superior corrosion resistance. However, as the grain size increases (Fig. 7c-d), the number and size of corrosion pits in the S1050 and S1150 samples increase, and corrosion expansion occurs on some pit walls, and the corrosion resistance decreases.

citation_sentences: TEM characterization was conducted to investigate the microstructural characteristics of the LDSs, and the results are shown in Fig. 5. The bright-field TEM image in Fig. 5a, along with the corresponding highresolution TEM (HRTEM) image and fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis (Fig. 5a ), confirms the presence of numerous κ-carbides (indicated by yellow arrows) with diameters ranging from 5 to 10 nm in the S0 sample. Fig. 5a reveals that the matrix of the LDSs is predominantly austenitic, and contains a high density of irregularly distributed dislocations in the S0 sample. As shown in Fig. 5b, the dislocation density decreases after solution treatment. Notably, dislocation tangles are observed adjacent to the ATBs, suggesting that the abundant ATBs effectively hinder dislocation movement.

citation_sentences: active dissolution region. From $- 0 . 6 7 \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } \mathrm { t } 0 \mathrm { ~ } { - 0 . 5 8 \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } }$ , the current density decreases, and the electrode reaction is in an unstable state, which indicates the surface oxide film formation. Subsequently, with the increase of potential, the current density remains relatively constant. This may be related to the formation of an oxide film with certain protective capabilities, and a similar passive phenomenon occurs. A breakdown current density $( { i _ { \mathrm { b r e } } } )$ derived from the polarization curves is shown in Fig. 6b. The lower breakdown current density indicates better corrosion resistance[38,39]. Notably, the error bars in this figure represent the standard deviation range of the tests. The error values of all the samples are controlled within 5 %, and the experimental data have good repeatability and reliability. The S0 sample without solution treatment has the highest $i _ { \mathrm { b r e } } ~ ( 9 . 9 2 ~ \mu \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 } )$ , and is relatively more prone to corrosion. The $\dot { \imath } _ { \mathrm { b r e } }$ of the fine-grained sample (S950) is 2.72 $\mathsf { \mu } \mathsf { A / c m } ^ { 2 } ,$ , which is lower than that of the coarse-grained sample (S1150) at 8.84 $\mu \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 }$ , and it has higher corrosion resistance.

citation_sentences: The EIS data of the LDSs were fitted via an appropriate equivalent circuit model. According to the Bode phase plot in Fig. 8c, the EIS results exhibit the superposition of two times constants, with a maximum phase angle between $6 5 ^ { \circ }$ and $7 5 ^ { \circ }$ at intermediate frequencies, characteristic of typical nonideal capacitive responses[41,42]. This deviation from the ideal capacitance arises from electrode surface porosity, inhomogeneity, and relaxation effects[43]. Consequently, the constant phase angle element Q is used in the equivalent circuit instead of the ideal capacitance C to account for these nonideal characteristics[44,45]. On the basis of the above analysis, the equivalent circuit diagram in Fig. 8a is employed for EIS fitting, where $R _ { s }$ is the solution resistance, $R _ { 1 }$ and $Q _ { 1 }$ are the diffusion resistance and constant phase angle element of ions at the solution/film interface, respectively, and $R _ { 2 }$ and $Q _ { 2 }$ are the oxide film resistance and constant phase angle element, respectively. The impedance of the two constant phase elements (CPEs), Q and $Q _ { 2 } ,$ in the equivalent circuit is defined as[46,47]:

paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: The corrosion resistance of the LDSs was further evaluated through potentiostatic polarization tests, and the relationship between the current density and time is presented in Fig. 10. During the initial 60 s of polarization, all samples exhibited a rapid current density decline to below ${ 9 \ \mu \mathrm { A } } / { \mathrm { c m } } ^ { 2 } ,$ corresponding to surface oxide film formation and initial anodic dissolution. This behavior confirms that all the samples can quickly form oxide films to inhibit anodic dissolution within a short period[49,50]. With increasing polarization time, the current density of all samples increases and then decreases during the polarization period of $6 0 0 { - } 1 4 0 0 s ,$ which corresponds to the rupture and self-healing process of the oxide film[51,52]. The S0 sample exhibits the highest stable current density $( 8 . 3 9 \mu \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 } )$ , which reflects relatively weak protection of its oxide film. With the increase of solution temperature, the grain size increases, and the stable current density of the LDSs gradually increases. The S950 sample achieves the lowest stable current density (2.45 $\mu \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 } )$ , outperforming both S1050 (4.52 $\mu \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 } )$ and S1150 (6.04 $\mu \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 } )$ . These results demonstrate that the oxide film of the S950 sample provides optimal corrosion protection.

paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: The EIS characterization of the oxide film on the LDSs after potentiostatic polarization for 7200 s in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution is presented in Fig. 11. The Nyquist plot in Fig. 11a reveals that the S0 sample has the smallest capacitive arc radius, indicating poor protective performance of its oxide film and higher corrosion susceptibility. The capacitance arc radius decreases with grain growth. The S950 sample has the largest radius, which confirms the superior density and stability of its oxide film. The Bode modulus plot in Fig. 11b indicates that the impedance modulus value at low frequency decreases with increasing grain size. The S950 sample maintains the highest low-frequency impedance, confirming the strong protective capability of its oxide film. The Bode phase plot (Fig. 11c) also shows a similar pattern. The S950 sample maintains a higher phase angle over a wider frequency range. These features demonstrate that its oxide film structure is more stable and uniform. The equivalent circuit diagram of Fig. 11a was used for EIS fitting, and the fitting results are shown in Table 3. The S950 sample achieves the highest $R _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ of $2 1 3 5 5 \ \Omega { \cdot } \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 }$ . Its oxide film has superior uniformity and density, which provide optimal protective properties.

caption: Fig. 12. (a) Mott-Schottky curves and (b) The $N _ { \mathrm { D } }$ and $N _ { \mathrm { A } }$ curves of LDSs in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: The Mott-Schottky analysis presented in Fig. 12 reveals the semiconductor characteristics and defect properties of oxide films formed on LDSs. As shown in Fig. 12a, all the samples demonstrate similar duplex semiconductor characteristics, exhibiting both n-type and p-type characteristics, across different potential regions. In Region 1 (-1.13 VSCE to $- 0 . 9 3 \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } )$ , the positive slope confirms that the n-type behavior is dominated by cation interstitials and oxygen vacancies. The slope of the curve in Region $2 \ ( - 0 . 9 3 \ \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } \ \mathrm { t o } \ - 0 . 6 7 \ \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } )$ shows negative slope characteristics indicative of p-type behavior, with cation vacancies as the primary defects. The n-type semiconductor characteristic of LDSs is mainly controlled by substances such as $\mathrm { { A l } _ { 2 } \mathrm { { O } _ { 3 } } }$ and $\mathrm { F e _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } }$ in the film[61, 62], whereas the p-type semiconductor characteristic is mainly controlled by oxides such as FeO[63,64]. The breakdown of the oxide film occurs at surface sites with high rates of cation vacancy diffusion and oxygen vacancy migration, where aggressive ions readily occupy these defect sites[65]. The carrier concentration in the oxide film of the LDSs is calculated by $\operatorname { E q . } \ ( 4 )$ and (5), and the $N _ { \mathrm { D } }$ and $N _ { \mathrm { A } }$ are shown in Fig. 12b. A lower $N _ { \mathrm { D } }$ and $N _ { \mathrm { A } }$ of the oxide film correspond to fewer structural defects and consequently reduced pathways for chloride ion penetration. The S0 sample exhibits the highest $N _ { \mathrm { D } } ~ ( 0 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ { 2 1 } ~ / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 } )$ and $N _ { \mathrm { A } } ~ ( 2 . 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ { 2 1 } ~ \mathrm { / c m ^ { 3 } } )$ values. This defective oxide film structure leads to inferior corrosion resistance. Although the solution treatment generally increases the $N _ { \mathrm { D } }$ and $N _ { \mathrm { A } }$ with increasing grain size, all the treated samples maintain lower values than the S0 sample. The S950 sample achieves the lowest $N _ { \mathrm { D } } ( 0 . 2 6 \times 1 0 ^ { 2 1 } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 } )$ and $\bar { N _ { \mathrm { A } } } \left( 0 . 6 8 \times 1 0 ^ { 2 1 } \right.$

Conversely, larger grain sizes correspond to lower GB densities, resulting in a sparsely distributed GB region that diminishes the number of reaction sites for oxide film formation during the initial stage of corrosion. This reduction in active sites results in a slower film formation rate and produces a more discontinuous oxide layer. Additionally, an increasing grain size correlates with a decreased Al content within the oxide film, producing a thinner and compositionally heterogeneous film exhibiting higher density of defects (Mott-Schottky results, Fig. 12). These defective regions may become channels for the infiltration of corrosive media, potentially leading to localized film breakdown and subsequent corrosion initiation[32,108,109].

citation_sentences: XPS analysis was performed to characterize the elemental composition and valence state distribution of the surface oxide film of the LDSs after 7200 s of potentiostatic polarization in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Fig. 13 shows the XPS spectra of Fe2p3/2, Mn2p3/2, Al2p, and O1s in the oxide film of the samples. According to the Fe2p3/2 spectra of the oxide film (Fig. 13a-d), Fe is composed of $\mathrm { F e _ { O X } ^ { 2 + } }$ (709.8 eV), $\mathrm { F e } _ { \mathrm { O X } } ^ { 3 + }$ (711.5 eV) and $\mathrm { F e _ { h y } ^ { 3 + } }$ (713.3 eV)[66–68]. Moreover, the S950 sample additionally exhibits a satellite peak $\mathrm { F e } _ { \mathrm { s a t } } ^ { 2 + }$ (715.0 eV), corresponding to the oxides FeO and $\mathrm { F e _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } }$ and the hydroxide FeOOH. Fig. 13a1-d1 shows the XPS spectra of Mn2p3/2 in the oxide film, where three peaks are detected in all the samples except for the S950 sample, composing of $\mathrm { M n ^ { 2 + } }$ (640.4 eV), $\mathrm { M n } ^ { \hat { 4 } + }$ (641.8 eV), and the satellite peak $\mathrm { M n } _ { \mathrm { s a t } } ^ { 2 + }$ $( 6 4 6 . 2 \ : \mathrm { e V } ) [ 6 9 - 7 1 ]$ , corresponding to Mn(OH)2 and $\mathrm { { M n O } } _ { 2 } .$ . The S950 sample uniquely displays only a $\mathrm { M n ^ { 2 + } }$ (640.4 eV) oxidation state. The Al2p spectra (Fig. 13a -d ) exhibit a single peak corresponding to $\mathsf { A l } ^ { 3 + }$ (74.1 eV)[22,72], which is attributed to Al oxide $\left( \mathsf { A l } _ { 2 } \mathsf { O } _ { 3 } \right)$ and hydroxide (Al(OH)3). According to the O1s spectra (Fig. 13a3-d3), the O in the surface oxide film consists of OH- (531.6 eV), H O (533.2 eV), and ${ \mathsf { O } } ^ { 2 \cdot }$ (529.7 eV)[67,68,70,71,73], corresponding to hydroxides, crystalline water, and oxides, respectively.

citation_sentences: The relative contents of different valence states of each metal in the oxide film of all the samples were calculated using Eq. (7), and are presented in Fig. 14.

where $\mathrm { I _ { X } }$ is the peak area corresponding to the element, and $\mathsf { s } _ { \mathrm { { X } } }$ represents the sensitivity factor of the XPS test. As shown in Fig. 14, Al emerges as the predominant metallic element in the oxide films of all the samples. Comparative analysis reveals that the S0 sample contains substantially higher Fe and Mn contents alongside a reduced Al content. The Al content progressively decreases with grain coarsening, accompanied by corresponding increases in the Fe and Mn contents, but the Al content in the samples after solution treatment is still higher than that in the S0 sample. The S950 sample has the highest content of Al in the oxide film, which is 90.17 at%. This behavior strongly indicates that grain refinement promotes the preferential dissolution and film formation of Al.

paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: AES analysis was used to characterize the elemental distribution within the oxide film along the depth profile. Fig. 15 illustrates the atomic concentration profiles of O, Fe, $\mathbf { A l } ,$ and Mn at various depths, which reveal oxygen enrichment at the surface of all samples with a gradual transition to the substrate composition at greater depths. The main components of the oxide film consist of Al and Fe oxides, and also contain trace amounts of Mn oxide. The oxide film thickness is conventionally determined as the depth at which the oxygen concentration decreases to half of its maximum value[74,75]. On the basis of this definition, the oxide film thickness of S0 is approximately 16.2 $\pm \ : 0 . 8$ nm and consists mainly of Fe, Al, and $^ { 0 , }$ with Fe and Al present in roughly comparable amounts. In comparison, the S950 sample has a significantly thicker film of $2 5 . 2 \pm 1 . 2 \mathrm { n m } ,$ , which is predominantly composed of uniformly distributed Al oxides. The Fe and Mn contents in the film are relatively low, which suggests a more compact and stable oxide film. The thicknesses of the S1050 and S1150 oxide films are 21.6 $\pm \nobreakspace 1 \nobreakspace$ nm and $1 8 \pm 0 . 9$ nm, respectively. Although the Al content is high in the outer film, with increasing sputtering depth, the Fe content increases and the Al content decreases. In summary, both the oxide film thickness and Al content tend to decrease with increasing grain size. Notably, compared with the other samples, the S950 sample forms a uniform, dense, and thick Al-rich oxide film.

caption: Fig. 16. E-pH diagram of the LDSs in Fe-Mn-Al-H2O system: (a) Al, (b) Fe, (c) Mn, and the pink circles represent the experimental conditions of this study.
paper_abstract: a National Materials Corrosion and Protection Data Center, State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metals and Materials, Institute for Advanced Materials and Technology, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China b Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing 100081, China c National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Functional Materials Processing, School of Metallurgical Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710055, China
paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: To comprehensively evaluate the stability of the Fe, Mn, and Al oxidation products under corrosive conditions, the E-pH diagram of the Fe-Mn-Al-H2O system at $2 5 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was calculated via HSC Chemistry 9 software. The calculation results are presented in Fig. 16. The results reveal that Al resides within the stable zone of $\mathrm { \bf A l _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } }$ under environmental conditions, which confirms its ability to spontaneously form a stable and dense oxide film even under anaerobic conditions. The main stable state of Fe is $\mathrm { F e ^ { 2 + } }$ , although the thermodynamic stability zone of FeO in the E-pH diagram (Fig. 16b) is located in the high pH and negative potential region. In the current environment, $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ is difficult to oxidize further because of the low oxygen concentration. Therefore, FeO remains the main oxide of Fe, with minor quantities of $\mathrm { F e _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } }$ and FeOOH potentially forming at the film defects or localized highpotential regions. The E-pH diagram of Mn (Fig. 16c) demonstrates that the minimal thermodynamic driving force for oxide formation under the current environmental conditions primarily exists as $\mathrm { M n ^ { 2 + } }$ . The stable regions of $\mathrm { M n ^ { 2 + } }$ and $\mathbf { M n O _ { 2 } }$ exhibit close proximity and comparable ranges, which suggests the thermodynamic feasibility of Mn oxidation to higher valence states within specific potential windows.

paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: To systematically investigate the differences in the oxide film formation rates among the various samples, the samples were immersed for periods ranging from 0.5 to 168 h under identical testing conditions, and the $R _ { \mathrm { p } }$ values were statistically analyzed via EIS measurements. The results are shown in Fig. 18. The fine-grained sample (S950) exhibits consistently higher $R _ { \mathrm { p } }$ values throughout the entire immersion period, reaching 114.9 kΩ⋅cm2 at 168 h, which is significantly higher than that of the coarse-grained sample (S1150) at 50.6 kΩ $\cdot \mathrm { c m } ^ { \dot { 2 } } .$ The $R _ { \mathrm { p } }$ value of the S950 sample increases rapidly during the initial stage of immersion (2–8 h) and then stabilizes after approximately 24 h. This indicates that a dense and stable oxide film forms rapidly on the surface of the S950 sample, which effectively protects the penetration of the corrosive medium. Conversely, the coarse-grained samples display lower initial $R _ { \mathrm { p } }$ values and slower growth rates with the extension of immersion time, suggesting delayed film formation and compromised structural integrity, which diminishes the protective efficacy. The variation among parallel samples is within 5 %, and the impedance evolution trend is consistent. The growth process of the oxide film has good repeatability and reproducibility in repeated experiments.

caption: Fig. 19. Cyclic voltammetry curves of the LDSs in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution: (a) first cycle; (b) second cycle.
paper_abstract: a National Materials Corrosion and Protection Data Center, State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metals and Materials, Institute for Advanced Materials and Technology, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China b Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing 100081, China c National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Functional Materials Processing, School of Metallurgical Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710055, China
paper_title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
citation_sentences: The Al content in the surface oxide films of the different LDSs significantly differed, as shown in Figs. 14 and 15. Notably, the S0 sample without solution treatment retains a substantial amount of κ-carbide, which limits the solid solubility of Al and yields the lowest Al content in its oxide film. Fig. 19a-b present the cyclic voltammetry curves of the LDSs in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The peak observed in the range $\mathbf { o f _ { \alpha } } - 0 . 8 1 \mathrm { ~ V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } { \sim } { - 0 . 7 7 \mathrm { ~ V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } }$ corresponds to Al oxidation, whereas the oxidation peak near approximately $- 0 . 5 6 \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } }$ represents Fe oxidation, possibly involving concurrent minor Mn oxidation.

In the first cycle (Fig. 19a), a distinct Al oxidation peak appears at approximately − 0.81 $\mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } } ,$ with the S950 sample exhibiting the highest peak current density. This observation suggests the superior oxidation activity of surface Al in the S950 sample, which facilitates its rapid participation in initial oxide film formation. Comparatively, the Al oxidation peaks of the S1050 and S1150 samples are noticeably weaker, which demonstrates lower Al oxidation reactivity on their surfaces. Furthermore, quantitative analysis of the Al oxidation peak region during the first cycle reveals that the charge density of the S950 sample reaches 2.55 $\times \dot { 1 } 0 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { C / c m } ^ { 2 }$ , which is higher than that of the S1050 $( 1 . 8 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { C / c m ^ { 2 } } )$ and S1150 (1.66 $\times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { C } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 } )$ samples. This indicates that the oxidation reaction of Al in the S950 is more complete in the initial stage, which promotes the rapid formation of an Al-rich oxide film. Notably, the characteristic Fe oxidation peak near − 0.56 $\mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { S C E } }$ is absent in the first cycle. This result has been consistently confirmed through repeated experiments (see Supporting Information Fig. S1). This phenomenon arises because preferential oxidation and rapid film formation of Al coat the Fe surface with an Al-rich oxide layer, thereby restricting reaction sites and suppressing Fe oxidation, which prevents formation of a detectable current peak during the first cycle.

In the second cycle (Fig. 19b), a distinct Fe oxidation peak emerges at approximately − 0.56 V alongside the persistent Al oxidation peak. The S950 sample displays the lowest peak intensity in this potential region. This result suggests the presence of a dense oxide film on the surface, which significantly inhibits the subsequent oxidation of Fe and Mn. Conversely, the S1050 and S1150 samples demonstrate substantially enhanced anodic peaks at this position, suggesting that the surface film is incompletely formed and that Fe and Mn continue to participate in the film formation process. The XPS results further confirm that the oxide film of the S950 sample contains a higher Al content coupled with reduced Fe and Mn concentrations compared to the other samples. This difference stems from the fine-grained structure and high GB density of the S950, which facilitate the preferential dissolution of Al at the GBs. Consequently, a continuous and dense Al-rich oxide film rapidly forms during the initial stages, effectively impinging subsequent Fe and Mn oxidation processes.
